name: Plasencia and the Honduras Pass Loop
name_original: Harvás desde Plasencia – circular por la Reserva Natural Garganta de los Infiernos
description: This difficult 70.1-mile road cycling route from Plasencia explores the scenic Garganta de los Infiernos Nature Reserve.
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Plasencia traverse diverse landscapes, from the river valleys of the Jerte to the protected areas of Monfragüe National Park. The region features varied terrain, including challenging ascents and more leisurely paths, catering to different fitness levels. Historical routes like the Vía de la Plata also offer extensive cycling opportunities. Best road cycling routes around Plasencia The most popular road cycling route is Plasencia and the Honduras Pass Loop, a 70.1 miles (112.8 km) trail that takes 5 hours 58 minutes to complete. This route features significant elevation gain and varied terrain. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Galisteo from Plasencia – circular by the Río Jerte, a moderate 27.0 miles (43.5 km) path. This route follows the course of the Río Jerte. Local road cyclists also love the Puerto de Piornal and La Vera Loop from Plasencia, a 61.7 miles (99.2 km) trail leading through mountainous terrain, often completed in about 5 hours 18 minutes. Road cycling around Plasencia is defined by river valleys, natural parks, and historical routes. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy rides to difficult ascents. The routes in Plasencia are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.4 stars from 12 reviews. More than 800 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Plasencia's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Plasencia?
A: Komoot offers nearly 50 road cycling routes around Plasencia, catering to various skill levels. You'll find everything from easy rides to challenging ascents through diverse landscapes.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on road cycling routes near Plasencia?
A: The region around Plasencia offers a wide variety of terrain. You can expect routes that traverse river valleys, such as those along the Río Jerte, as well as more challenging mountainous sections with significant elevation gains, particularly in areas like the Honduras Pass or the Sierra de Gredos foothills. There are also flatter sections, including greenways like the Vía Verde del Monfragüe.

Q: When is the best time of year for road cycling in Plasencia?
A: The spring months, particularly March and April, are highly recommended for road cycling in Plasencia, especially to witness the stunning cherry blossoms in the Jerte Valley. Autumn also offers pleasant temperatures and beautiful scenery. Summers can be hot, but early morning rides are still enjoyable, and you can cool off in natural swimming pools in the Jerte Valley.

Q: Are there any easy road cycling routes suitable for beginners in Plasencia?
A: Yes, Plasencia offers several easy road cycling routes perfect for beginners or those seeking a more relaxed ride. An example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1056104963/plasencia-jerte-river-and-reservoir-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Plasencia: Jerte River and Reservoir Loop&lt;/a&gt;, which covers a shorter distance with minimal elevation gain, allowing you to enjoy the riverside scenery.

Q: What natural features or landmarks can I see while road cycling around Plasencia?
A: Road cycling around Plasencia offers access to stunning natural features. You can ride through the biodiverse Monfragüe National Park, known for its birdwatching opportunities, or explore the scenic Jerte Valley, famous for its cherry blossoms and natural swimming pools. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5391554" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Embalse de Plasencia&lt;/a&gt; and other reservoirs also provide picturesque views along many routes.

Q: Are there any historical sites accessible by road bike near Plasencia?
A: Absolutely. Plasencia is rich in history. You can cycle along sections of the ancient Vía de la Plata, a historic Roman road. Within Plasencia itself, you can visit the impressive &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6517874" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Plasencia Cathedral&lt;/a&gt; and the historic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6517887" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Plaza Mayor&lt;/a&gt;. Further afield, the Roman &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/684512" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Arco de Cáparra&lt;/a&gt; is another significant landmark that can be incorporated into longer rides.

Q: Are there challenging road cycling routes for experienced riders in Plasencia?
A: Experienced road cyclists will find plenty of challenging routes around Plasencia. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1056110324/plasencia-and-the-honduras-pass-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Plasencia and the Honduras Pass Loop&lt;/a&gt; is a demanding 112 km route with significant elevation gain. Another option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1292411084/puerto-de-piornal-and-la-vera-loop-from-plasencia" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puerto de Piornal and La Vera Loop from Plasencia&lt;/a&gt;, which takes you through mountainous terrain.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy the most about road cycling in Plasencia?
A: The road cycling routes in Plasencia are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.4 stars from over 12 reviews. Cyclists often praise the region's diverse landscapes, from river valleys to natural parks, and the variety of routes that cater to different ability levels. Many appreciate the well-documented paths and the opportunity to explore both natural beauty and historical sites.

Q: Are there circular road cycling routes available from Plasencia?
A: Yes, many road cycling routes around Plasencia are designed as circular loops, making them convenient for starting and ending in the city. An example is the moderate &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1058638079/galisteo-from-plasencia-circular-by-the-rio-jerte" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Galisteo from Plasencia – circular by the Río Jerte&lt;/a&gt;, which follows the river's course. Other longer loops, like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1058620553/plasencia-and-monfraguee-national-park-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Plasencia and Monfragüe National Park Loop&lt;/a&gt;, also offer a circular experience.

Q: Are there any family-friendly road cycling options in the Plasencia area?
A: While many road cycling routes are geared towards more experienced riders, families can find suitable options, especially on greenways or flatter sections. The Vía Verde del Monfragüe offers a picturesque and generally easy cycling experience on an old railway line, perfect for a relaxed family outing through nature. Always check the route difficulty and elevation profile to ensure it matches your family's capabilities.

Q: Are there specific areas for parking when accessing road cycling trails in Plasencia?
A: Plasencia itself serves as an excellent base, and you can often find parking within the city to start your rides. For routes venturing into areas like Monfragüe National Park or the Jerte Valley, there are usually designated parking areas at trailheads or visitor centers. It's advisable to check specific route descriptions on komoot for recommended starting points and parking information.
tips: Here is a nice little town. The population does not reach 900 inhabitants but it has a hostel for cyclists. It is a good place to start an excursion or to stop during an excursion.

Hervás is a small regional town to the north of Cásares in the Ambroz Valley. Surrounded by mountains, the town is located on the Ambroz river. It has one of the best preserved Jewish quarters in Spain.

Spectacular views from this mountain pass. The Port of Honduras is located at 1440 meters above sea level on the CC-120 highway. The ascent from Hervás has about 17km gaining 920m with an average gradient of 5.2%
Great views of the Jerte River, next to the El Vao beach bar and the children's playground. It is an ideal resting place.
Spectacular views of the Plasencia Reservoir and the valley of the Jerte River below. The path passes next to the river dam.

The Plasencia Aqueduct or the Arches of San Antón is not of Roman origin as many Placentines believe, but rather dates back to the 16th century and was built by the great architect Juan de Flandes.
55 arches are preserved from it and with it the Muslim clay pipe that carried water from the Cabezabellosa and El Torno mountains was replaced.
in https://plasenciaturismo.es/acueducto

The city of Plasencia is blessed with two cathedrals. One from the 13th century and one from the 15th century. The idea was to demolish the old one when the new one was finished, but that never happened.

faqs.question: Is the 'Hiking from Plasencia – circular through the Garganta de los Infiernos Nature Reserve' suitable for road cycling?
faqs.answer: Despite its listed sport, this route is primarily designed for hiking and is generally not suitable for road cycling. The terrain includes unpaved sections and significant elevation changes that would be challenging or impossible for a road bike. While the region offers excellent road cycling opportunities, this specific trail is best experienced on foot.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this circular hike?
faqs.answer: The terrain is varied, featuring paths through forests, ascending routes, and unpaved sections. You can expect to encounter natural pools and waterfalls, particularly at Los Pilones, along with areas shaded by chestnut and oak trees. It's a challenging route with significant elevation changes.

faqs.question: What natural features and landmarks can I see along the route?
faqs.answer: This route takes you through the stunning Garganta de los Infiernos Nature Reserve, known for its natural pools and waterfalls, especially at Los Pilones. You'll also pass by the Honduras Pass and follow sections near the Jerte River. The trail offers viewpoints of waterfalls and passes through beautiful forests of chestnut and oak trees.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the trail?
faqs.answer: Specific parking information for this exact circular route from Plasencia is not detailed. However, for accessing the Garganta de los Infiernos Nature Reserve, parking is typically available near visitor centers or popular access points within the reserve itself. If starting directly from Plasencia, you would need to find suitable parking within the city before beginning your hike.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit or is there an entrance fee for the Garganta de los Infiernos Nature Reserve?
faqs.answer: Generally, there is no entrance fee to access the Garganta de los Infiernos Nature Reserve itself for hiking. However, it's always advisable to check local regulations for any specific areas or attractions within the reserve that might require a small fee or permit, especially during peak seasons. No specific permits are mentioned for this particular hiking route.

faqs.question: Is this trail dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While many natural areas in Spain are dog-friendly, specific regulations for dogs within the Garganta de los Infiernos Nature Reserve can vary. It's common for dogs to be allowed on trails, but often they must be kept on a leash to protect wildlife and ensure the safety of other hikers. Always check local signage upon arrival for the most current rules.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to hike this route?
faqs.answer: The best time to hike this route largely depends on your preferences. Spring (April-May) offers lush greenery and blooming wildflowers, while autumn (October-November) provides spectacular fall colors from the chestnut and oak trees. Summer can be very hot, making early mornings or late afternoons preferable, especially if you plan to enjoy the natural pools. Winter can be cold, but offers a different, quieter experience.

faqs.question: Are there any wild swimming spots along the route?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Garganta de los Infiernos Nature Reserve is famous for its natural pools and waterfalls, particularly at Los Pilones. These natural formations offer opportunities for wild swimming, especially during warmer months. Always exercise caution and be aware of water levels and currents.

faqs.question: Does this trail intersect with other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route incorporates sections of several other paths. Notably, it covers parts of the [CIMA CC10] Honduras * Valle del Jerte, [CIMA CC10] Honduras * Hervás, and the Camino Mozárabe por Trujillo. You may also encounter segments of the Mtb Vía de la Plata and the Vía de la Plata hiking - 02 Merida to Salamanca.

faqs.question: What should I bring for this difficult hike?
faqs.answer: Given the trail's difficulty and length, you should bring plenty of water, snacks, appropriate hiking footwear with good ankle support, and layers of clothing suitable for changing weather conditions. Sun protection (hat, sunscreen) is essential, as is a map or GPS device. Trekking poles can be helpful for the varied terrain and elevation changes.

faqs.question: Are there places to eat or stay near the trail?
faqs.answer: Starting from Plasencia, you'll find a wide range of accommodation, cafes, and restaurants. As you venture into the Garganta de los Infiernos Nature Reserve, options become more limited, but nearby towns like Hervás Old Town or those within the Jerte Valley offer amenities. It's advisable to plan your stops, especially for food and drink, as services directly on the trail are scarce.
